sectionName: Deduction in respect of contribution to National Pension System (NPS)
maxLimit: 80CCD(1): ₹1,50,000 within combined 80C cap | 80CCD(1B): ₹50,000 additional | 80CCD(2): 14% of salary (no monetary ceiling)
description: Section 80CCD covers three distinct NPS-related deductions: 80CCD(1) for the employee's own contribution within the ₹1.5L cap, 80CCD(1B) for an additional ₹50,000 over and above the ₹1.5L cap (old regime only), and 80CCD(2) for the employer's NPS contribution which is available in BOTH old and new tax regimes. The employer contribution limit was raised to 14% of salary for private-sector employees from FY 2025-26.
eligibleAssessees: HUF (not eligible for 80CCD(2) — employer contribution applies to individuals only)
notEligible: Taxpayers opting for new regime for 80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B)
NPS Tier 2 account holders (Tier 2 has no tax benefit under any sub-section)

eligibleInvestments.name: 80CCD(1) — Employee's own NPS Tier 1 contribution (salaried)
eligibleInvestments.limit: 10% of salary (Basic + DA). Within ₹1,50,000 combined cap of 80C + 80CCC + 80CCD(1). Old regime only.
eligibleInvestments.notes: Salary means Basic Pay + Dearness Allowance. HRA, special allowances, and other perquisites are excluded. The deduction is capped at the lower of (a) actual contribution or (b) 10% of salary. Deduction available only under old regime.

eligibleInvestments.name: 80CCD(1) — Self-employed individual's own NPS Tier 1 contribution
eligibleInvestments.limit: 20% of Gross Total Income (GTI). Within ₹1,50,000 combined cap. Old regime only.
eligibleInvestments.notes: Self-employed individuals (professionals, proprietors, freelancers) can claim up to 20% of GTI (before this deduction). This is a higher percentage than salaried employees to compensate for no employer contribution.

eligibleInvestments.name: 80CCD(1B) — Additional NPS Tier 1 contribution (over and above ₹1.5L cap)
eligibleInvestments.limit: ₹50,000 per year. This is OVER AND ABOVE the ₹1,50,000 combined cap of 80C + 80CCC + 80CCD(1). Old regime only.
eligibleInvestments.notes: This is a separate, additional deduction available to both salaried and self-employed individuals. Even if the ₹1.5L cap is fully exhausted by 80C investments, an additional ₹50,000 can be claimed under 80CCD(1B) by contributing to NPS Tier 1. Maximum tax saving: ₹15,600 at 30% slab + 4% cess.

eligibleInvestments.name: 80CCD(2) — Employer's NPS contribution (available in BOTH regimes)
eligibleInvestments.limit: 14% of salary (Basic + DA) for private-sector employees (raised from 10% in FY 2025-26). 14% for Central Government employees (unchanged). No upper monetary ceiling.
eligibleInvestments.notes: Employer contributes to employee's NPS Tier 1 account as part of CTC. This deduction is available in BOTH old and new tax regimes — a critical advantage. The Finance Act 2025 raised the limit for private-sector employers from 10% to 14% of salary, bringing parity with Central Government employees. Amount appears in Form 16 Part B.

conditions: NPS Tier 1 account (Permanent Retirement Account) is mandatory — deduction is NOT available for Tier 2 contributions.
80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B) are available only under the old tax regime. Taxpayers opting for new regime cannot claim these.
80CCD(2) is available in BOTH old and new tax regimes — even taxpayers under new regime can claim employer NPS contribution deduction.
80CCD(2) limit for private employers is now 14% of (Basic + DA) effective FY 2025-26 under Finance Act 2025.
80CCD(1B) ₹50,000 is an additional deduction fully independent of the ₹1.5L cap — both can be claimed simultaneously.
For 80CCD(2): only employer contribution made to a notified pension scheme (NPS) qualifies. EPF or Superannuation fund contributions fall under different sections.
Employees must have a PRAN (Permanent Retirement Account Number) issued by NPS Trust.
NPS maturity: 60% lump sum is exempt from tax; 40% must be used to purchase an annuity (pension) which is taxable as salary income in the year of receipt.
documentationRequired: NPS Tier 1 Transaction Statement for the financial year
Form 16 Part B — showing employer's contribution under Section 80CCD(2)
Salary slips showing employee's NPS deduction (for 80CCD(1))
Bank account statement or challan for voluntary additional NPS contribution (for 80CCD(1B))
NSDL CRA / KFintech NPS portal transaction history

keyRules.rule: 80CCD(2) employer contribution limit raised from 10% to 14% of salary (Basic + DA) for private-sector employees effective FY 2025-26. This is a Finance Act 2025 change bringing parity with Central Government employees.
keyRules.legalReference: Section 80CCD(2) as amended by Finance Act 2025

keyRules.rule: Section 80CCE restricts the combined deduction under 80C + 80CCC + 80CCD(1) to ₹1,50,000. Section 80CCD(1B) ₹50,000 and Section 80CCD(2) are explicitly excluded from this cap — they are genuinely additional.
80CCD(2) is the only NPS-related deduction available in the new tax regime. All other NPS deductions (80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B)) require the old regime.
keyRules.legalReference: Section 115BAC(2) — list of deductions available in new regime

keyRules.rule: Upon NPS maturity at age 60: the 60% lump sum withdrawn is exempt from tax. The remaining 40% must mandatorily be used to purchase an annuity from an annuity service provider; the pension received from this annuity is taxable as 'Income from Salaries' in the year of receipt.
keyRules.legalReference: Section 10(12A) — exemption of 60% NPS withdrawal; Section 17(1)(ia) — pension from annuity

keyRules.rule: For self-employed individuals, the 80CCD(1) deduction is capped at 20% of Gross Total Income (not 10% as for salaried). There is no employer contribution available to self-employed; hence, a higher own-contribution limit is provided.

Employer NPS contribution is first included in the employee's gross salary and then deducted under 80CCD(2). This means it must appear in Form 16 and the ITR — omitting it from gross salary and also not claiming deduction would both be incorrect.

commonMistakes: Confusing 80CCD(1B) ₹50,000 as part of the ₹1.5L cap — it is a separate additional deduction over and above the cap, giving a total potential NPS-related deduction of ₹2,00,000.
Claiming 80CCD(2) at the old 10% limit for private employers instead of the new 14% limit effective FY 2025-26 — this results in a missed deduction.
Not claiming 80CCD(2) at all while filing under the new regime — many taxpayers incorrectly believe no Chapter VI-A deductions are available in the new regime, but 80CCD(2) is specifically allowed.
Claiming employer's NPS contribution under 80CCD(1) instead of 80CCD(2) — these are different sub-sections with different rules and regime applicability.

Contributing to NPS Tier 2 and trying to claim 80C or 80CCD deductions — Tier 2 has no tax deduction benefit (though NPS Tier 2 for government employees has a 3-year lock-in benefit for 80C under specific scheme).
Not filing Form 10-IEA (for business income filers) before the due date when opting for old regime — failure to file means new regime is treated as default and 80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B) are lost.
Omitting employer NPS contribution from gross salary — the employer contribution must be added to salary income first, then deducted under 80CCD(2).
relatedSections: 80CCC — Contribution to annuity/pension fund of LIC or other insurer
Section 10(12A) — Exemption of 60% NPS lump sum withdrawal on maturity
Section 10(12B) — Exemption on NPS partial withdrawal
Section 115BAC — New tax regime (80CCD(2) available; 80CCD(1) and 80CCD(1B) not available)

workedExample.profile: Salaried employee, Basic salary ₹15,00,000. Employer contributes 14% NPS = ₹2,10,000. Employee own NPS contribution = ₹1,50,000 (within 80C cap). Additional NPS contribution = ₹50,000 (80CCD(1B)). Under new regime: Only 80CCD(2) ₹2,10,000 deductible. Under old regime: 80CCD(2) ₹2,10,000 + 80CCD(1) ₹1,50,000 (within ₹1.5L cap) + 80CCD(1B) ₹50,000 = ₹4,10,000 total NPS-related deduction.
workedExample.taxSaving: Old regime vs new regime NPS benefit: Additional ₹2,00,000 (80CCD(1) + 80CCD(1B)) at 30% slab = ₹60,000 + 4% cess = ₹62,400 additional saving in old regime. Under new regime, ₹2,10,000 (80CCD(2)) at applicable slab rate saves approximately ₹63,000 + cess at 30% slab.
